summary: Arkansas DHS states that Arkansas Works was replaced by Arkansas Health and Opportunity for Me (ARHOME) on January 1, 2022. ARHOME uses Medicaid dollars to buy private health insurance for eligible beneficiaries.

source_name: Arkansas Department of Human Services: ARHOME
scraped_text_excerpt: questions about eligibility  should contact the Division of County Operations . On January 1, 2022, Arkansas Works was replaced by the Arkansas Health and Opportunity for Me program, or ARHOME . The ARHOME program uses Medicaid dollars to buy private health insurance for you. You’re still covered by Medicaid, but your coverage is provided by one of two private health insurance carriers – Blue Cross Blue Shield or Ambetter . ARHOME focuses on improving your health, not just providing reliable health care coverage. ARHOME also
proposed_blurb: Arkansas policy context includes ARHOME, which replaced Arkansas Works on January 1, 2022. This state-specific waiver structure may help contextualize Arkansas enrollment and operations trends, but the dashboard does not estimate its causal effect.

event_title: North Carolina Medicaid expansion implemented
summary: NC Medicaid states that on December 1, 2023, North Carolina expanded Medicaid to cover people ages 19 through 64 up to 138% of the federal poverty line.
dashboard_relevance: This in-period expansion directly affects eligibility and may help contextualize North Carolina enrollment, applications, and state comparisons after December 2023.
source_name: NC Medicaid: North Carolina Expands Medicaid
scraped_text_excerpt: erage to more people. Versión en español Video in American Sign Language (ASL)  On December 1, 2023, NC Medicaid expanded to cover people ages 19 through 64 up to 138% of the federal poverty line. That’s about $1,800/month for singles and $3,065/month for families of three. NC Medicaid covers most health services. It includes doctor visits, check-ups, emergency care, dental care, vision and hearing services, prescription drugs, maternity and postpartum care, hospital services, behavioral health, preventive and wellness servi
proposed_blurb: North Carolina expanded Medicaid on December 1, 2023, covering adults ages 19 through 64 up to 138% of the federal poverty line. This may help contextualize North Carolina enrollment and application patterns after late 2023 without implying causality.

event_title: Georgia Pathways to Coverage with qualifying activities
summary: Georgia Pathways says it offers Medicaid coverage to eligible Georgians ages 19-64 up to 100% FPL who are not otherwise eligible for traditional Medicaid and meet qualifying activity requirements, including 80 hours per month to maintain coverage.
dashboard_relevance: This state-specific limited coverage pathway and activity-reporting design may help contextualize Georgia enrollment and eligibility operations compared with full expansion states.
source_name: Georgia Pathways to Coverage: About Pathways

For example, in 2026, this equals $15,960 per year or $1,330 on average per month for one person, and $27,320 per year or $2,277 on average per month for a family of three. Eligible Georgians are not otherwise eligible for traditional Medicaid and meet the qualifying activities threshold. Qualifying activity requ
proposed_blurb: Georgia policy context includes Pathways to Coverage, a limited Medicaid coverage pathway for some adults up to 100% FPL with qualifying activity requirements. This may help contextualize Georgia trends, but it should not be treated as full ACA expansion.

event_title: Nebraska Medicaid expansion / Heritage Health Adult benefits
summary: Nebraska DHHS states Medicaid coverage is available for Nebraskans age 19-64 up to 138% FPL, and that everyone eligible through expansion automatically received equal benefits effective October 1, 2021.
dashboard_relevance: In-period benefit/equalization change may help contextualize Nebraska expansion enrollment and program interpretation.

event_title: Utah full Medicaid expansion authorized
summary: Utah DHHS states CMS authorized Utah to implement full Medicaid expansion on December 23, 2019, extending eligibility to adults up to 138% FPL; the page also notes the community engagement requirement was withdrawn in 2021 after suspension during the COVID-19 public health emergency.
dashboard_relevance: This in-period expansion and withdrawal of community engagement policy may help contextualize Utah enrollment and eligibility operations after 2020.
source_name: Utah DHHS Medicaid: Medicaid Expansion

Program History On December 23, 2019, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) authorized the Utah Department of Health (UDOH) to implement a full Medicaid expansion in the state. The expansion extends Medicaid eligibility to Utah adults whose annual income is up to 138% of the federal poverty level ($17,608 for an individual or $36,156 for a family of four). The federal government covers 90% of the costs for these services, with the state covering t
proposed_blurb: Utah policy context includes CMS authorization of full Medicaid expansion in December 2019 and later withdrawal of the community engagement requirement. This may help contextualize Utah trends during the dashboard period without implying a causal estimate.

event_title: AHCCCS 1115 waiver extension approved with Housing and Health Opportunities
summary: CMS approved Arizona’s five-year Section 1115 waiver extension on October 14, 2022. AHCCCS states the extension continued integrated managed care authorities and approved transformative projects including Targeted Investments 2.0 and Housing and Health Opportunities.
dashboard_relevance: This may help contextualize Arizona enrollment and eligibility operations as part of a managed-care-centered Medicaid program with waiver features tied to care integration and housing-related supports.
source_name: Arizona Health Care Cost Containment System (AHCCCS)
scraped_text_excerpt: On 10/14/22, CMS approved Arizona’s request for a five-year extension of its 1115 Waiver... including integrated managed care... Targeted Investments 2.O and Housing and Health Opportunities.
proposed_blurb: Arizona policy context includes a CMS-approved 2022 AHCCCS 1115 waiver extension that continued integrated managed care authorities and added Housing and Health Opportunities. This may help contextualize Arizona enrollment and operations trends without implying causality.
notes: Fourth-pass Batch 2 official-source review. State-specific official source verified. KFF and Medicaid.gov State Profiles may be used only as eligibility/background support.

event_title: MassHealth 1115 Demonstration extension approved through 2027
summary: Mass.gov states that CMS approved Massachusetts’ request to extend the MassHealth Section 1115 Demonstration through December 2027, continuing a demonstration used to support coverage, the safety net, behavioral health services, and delivery-system reform.
dashboard_relevance: This may help contextualize Massachusetts enrollment, program structure, and operations because MassHealth uses a broad 1115 demonstration as a central program authority.
source_name: Mass.gov / MassHealth
scraped_text_excerpt: On September 28, 2022, the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) approved Massachusetts’ request to extend the MassHealth Section 1115 Demonstration Waiver... through December 2027.
proposed_blurb: Massachusetts policy context includes a CMS-approved MassHealth 1115 Demonstration extension through 2027. The waiver is a major program authority for coverage, safety-net, behavioral health, and delivery-system initiatives, so enrollment trends should be read alongside that structure.
notes: Fourth-pass Batch 2 official-source review. State-specific official source verified. Does not claim waiver caused observed enrollment changes.

event_title: OHP Bridge launched for adults with incomes above traditional OHP Plus limits
summary: Oregon Health Authority describes OHP Bridge as a new Oregon Health Plan benefit starting July 1, 2024 for adults with income above traditional OHP Plus limits and between 133% and 200% FPL.
dashboard_relevance: This is directly relevant to enrollment and program mix interpretation because it created a new coverage pathway for adults just above traditional OHP limits.
source_name: Oregon Health Authority
scraped_text_excerpt: OHP Bridge is a new health coverage benefit for adults with income just above the traditional OHP Plus limit... Starting July 1, 2024...
proposed_blurb: Oregon policy context includes OHP Bridge, launched July 1, 2024 for adults with income above traditional OHP Plus limits and up to 200% FPL. This may help contextualize Oregon enrollment patterns around mid-2024 without implying causality.
notes: Fourth-pass Batch 2 official-source review. Strong state-specific eligibility expansion candidate.

event_title: Apple Health Expansion limited-enrollment coverage pathway
summary: Washington HCA describes Apple Health Expansion as a limited-enrollment Apple Health program. The official page notes the enrollment limit has been met, that no immigration documentation is needed if undocumented, and that pregnancy-related Apple Health coverage is available regardless of citizenship or immigration status.
dashboard_relevance: This may help contextualize Washington enrollment and application activity because the state created a limited-enrollment coverage pathway for immigrant adults, with enrollment-cap constraints.
source_name: Washington State Health Care Authority
scraped_text_excerpt: Apple Health Expansion is one of the few Apple Health programs with limited enrollment... the enrollment limit has been met... No immigration documentation is needed if you are undocumented.
proposed_blurb: Washington policy context includes Apple Health Expansion, a limited-enrollment Apple Health pathway for people who may otherwise face immigration-related coverage barriers. Enrollment-cap rules may help contextualize application and enrollment patterns without implying causality.
notes: Fourth-pass Batch 2 official-source review. Launch month inferred from state announcement/page context; source clearly verifies program and enrollment-cap status.

event_title: ForwardHealth annual renewal process and late-renewal window
summary: Wisconsin DHS explains that BadgerCare Plus and other Wisconsin Medicaid programs require annual renewal. The page describes administrative renewal when current information is available, renewal packets when more information is needed, and a late-renewal window for most programs up to three months after the due date.
dashboard_relevance: This is useful operations context for interpreting Wisconsin application, determination, and renewal-related enrollment patterns, especially because renewal timing and late-renewal rules can affect monthly operations measures.
source_name: Wisconsin Department of Health Services
scraped_text_excerpt: This process is called a renewal... If we do and you still meet the rules... benefits will be renewed... If you don’t renew during your renewal month, your benefits will end... late renewal... up to three months later.
proposed_blurb: Wisconsin policy context includes a detailed ForwardHealth renewal process, including administrative renewal when information is available and a late-renewal window for most Medicaid programs. This may help contextualize monthly operations and enrollment changes without treating them as performance scores.
notes: Fourth-pass Batch 2 official-source review. Official state operations/renewal context; not a new eligibility expansion.
